title: Chilaquiles Red Salsa
description: Chilaquiles Rojos are a quintessential Mexican breakfast, a symphony of textures and flavors. Crispy corn tortilla pieces are simmered in a vibrant, smoky red salsa made from dried chiles and ripe tomatoes, softening to a delightful chewiness. Topped with fresh crema, crumbly queso fresco, and cilantro, it's a comforting and robust dish that awakens the senses.

instructions.title: Prepare the Chiles
instructions.text: Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat. Toast the guajillo and chile de árbol (if using) for 30-60 seconds per side, until fragrant but not burnt. Remove from heat, place in a bowl, and cover with hot water. Let them rehydrate for 15-20 minutes until soft.

instructions.title: Cook the Aromatics
instructions.text: While chiles rehydrate, in the same comal or skillet, lightly char the Roma tomatoes, quarter white onion, and garlic cloves over medium-high heat. This step adds a smoky depth to the salsa. Alternatively, you can boil them in water until softened.

instructions.title: Blend the Salsa
instructions.text: Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving about 1/2 cup of the soaking water. Transfer the chiles, charred tomatoes, onion, garlic, cumin, and 3 cups of chicken broth (or water) to a blender. Blend until completely smooth. If needed, add a little of the reserved chile water to achieve a smooth consistency.

instructions.title: Strain the Salsa (Optional)
instructions.text: For a smoother salsa with a more refined texture, pass the blended mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a bowl, pressing down on the solids with a spoon to extract all the liquid. Discard the remaining pulp. This step is highly recommended for restaurant-quality results.

instructions.title: Fry the Totopos (Tortilla Chips)
instructions.text: Heat about 1 inch of vegetable oil in a large, heavy-bottomed skillet or Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Fry the corn tortilla triangles in batches until golden brown and crispy, about 2-3 minutes per batch. Remove with a slotted spoon and place on a wire rack lined with paper towels to drain excess oil. Season lightly with salt.

instructions.title: Simmer the Salsa
instructions.text: In a separate large pot or deep skillet, heat 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil over medium heat. Pour in the strained salsa. Bring to a simmer, then reduce heat to low and cook for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the salsa has thickened slightly and the flavors have melded. If the salsa becomes too thick, add the remaining 1 cup of chicken broth. Season generously with salt.

instructions.title: Combine and Serve
instructions.text: Just before serving, add the fried totopos to the simmering salsa. Gently stir to coat all the chips. Cook for 1-2 minutes, or until the chips are softened but still have a slight bite (adjust cooking time to your preferred texture).

instructions.title: Garnish and Enjoy
instructions.text: Immediately transfer the chilaquiles to individual plates. Drizzle generously with Mexican crema, sprinkle with crumbled queso fresco, and garnish with chopped fresh cilantro and finely diced white onion (if using). Serve hot and enjoy this authentic Mexican breakfast!

tips: Always toast your dried chiles lightly before rehydrating to enhance their smoky flavor.
For the best texture, fry your own totopos from day-old corn tortillas; they absorb less oil.
Add the totopos to the salsa just before serving to prevent them from becoming too soggy.
Serve with a fried egg on top for a richer, more complete breakfast experience.
article:
## History of Chilaquiles Red Salsa

Chilaquiles, pronounced chee-lah-KEE-lehs, are a dish steeped in Mexican culinary tradition, with roots tracing back to the Aztec civilization. The name itself comes from the Nahuatl word "chil-a-quilitl," meaning "chiles and greens." Originally, it was a practical way to use leftover tortillas, transforming them into a hearty and flavorful meal. While the specific form of chilaquiles we know today likely evolved during the colonial era with the introduction of new ingredients and cooking methods, the core concept of simmering corn tortillas in a chile-based sauce remains ancient. It's a testament to Mexican ingenuity, turning humble ingredients into a beloved staple, particularly as a comforting breakfast or brunch.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe excels due to its authentic approach to flavor building. Toasting the dried chiles before rehydrating them unlocks their deep, smoky notes and complex fruitiness, which are then further enhanced by the charring of the tomatoes, onion, and garlic. Blending and then simmering the salsa allows the flavors to fully meld and deepen, creating a rich, robust base. Frying the tortilla chips separately ensures they develop a crispy texture that, when introduced to the salsa, softens perfectly without becoming mushy too quickly. The combination of savory salsa, textural chips, and cooling, tangy toppings creates a harmonious and satisfying dish that embodies the soul of Mexican breakfast.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

For the best flavor, always toast your dried chiles lightly before rehydrating; this brings out their essential oils and deepens their taste. Don't rush the simmering of the salsa; allow it to cook down for at least 15-20 minutes to thicken and intensify its flavors. When frying your totopos, use day-old corn tortillas as they contain less moisture and will crisp up better. And crucially, add the totopos to the salsa *just before serving* to control their texture – some prefer them softer, others with a bit more bite. Always serve immediately to enjoy the perfect balance of textures.

## Serving Suggestions

Chilaquiles Red Salsa is a complete meal on its own, but it truly shines when paired with traditional Mexican accompaniments. A fried egg, sunny-side up, is a classic addition, allowing its runny yolk to enrich the salsa. For a heartier breakfast, serve alongside refried beans or a side of thinly sliced carne asada. A sprinkle of crumbled cotija cheese can replace queso fresco for a saltier, sharper note. Don't forget a side of fresh avocado slices or a simple pico de gallo to add a refreshing counterpoint to the rich salsa. A glass of agua fresca or strong Mexican coffee completes the experience.

jsonLd.keywords: Mexican, Mexican, authentic, traditional, breakfast, Mexican, authentic, traditional, breakfast, chilaquiles, salsa roja
recipeIngredient: 4-6 Chile de Árbol, dried (stemmed, optional for heat)
1 tbsp Vegetable oil (plus more for frying tortillas)
12-15 Corn tortillas, day-old (cut into quarters or triangles)
